title: 'Pavitos' — Sept 2021 title-only snack-name capture
html:
TL;DR

  • Title-only Apple Note from Sept 3, 2021 — Yahir is 18, four weeks before his freshman move to OSU in Corvallis. Title is the entire artifact: "Pavitos." Body is empty. 7-second create→modify gap.
  • Pavitos are the Mexican savory-snack-stick brand (corn-and-flour fried sticks, often chile-lime flavored) — staple in Mexican-American households. The single-word title reads as either a grocery-list item Yahir wanted to remember to buy, a craving, or a memory marker from a Grants Pass family pantry moment.
  • Pure single-word capture — the title is a noun, the meaning is whatever was in Yahir's head at 5:24pm. Compare with the "Paper towel holder" note from a month later (also single-word-noun-as-title with brief shopping body).
  • Sits in the fall-2021 pre-OSU material-life cluster: shopping lists, dorm-prep, the Ford prep todos — Yahir at 18 capturing the small-stuff details of moving out of the family house.
  • Likely connects to the Mexican American family food register threaded through later notes (2022 09 13, the mom-cooking memos) — Pavitos is one of those identity-markers that Yahir doesn't gloss because it doesn't need glossing inside the head he's writing to.

Source

"Pavitos"

Why it matters in the graph

  • An example of single-word-noun titles in the corpus where the missing context is itself the data — the note assumes a reader (future-Yahir) who will reconstruct the meaning from his own pantry memory.

Open questions

  • Grocery-list item, snack craving, or pantry-of-the-childhood-house mnemonic?
  • Did Pavitos show up in any later note? No corpus matches found.
